skill.name: chart-generator
skill.description: Create static and interactive charts from structured data using common Python and JavaScript visualization libraries.
skill.summary: Generate charts, dashboards, exports, and visualization guidance from structured data.
skill.author: CuriousLearner

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high-risk matches, but review found the command execution, weak cryptography, environment access, and reconnaissance matches are false positives from Markdown fences or ordinary chart text. The only confirmed risk is expected filesystem output behavior for generated chart files, so publication is acceptable with normal file path caution.

low_findings.title: Caller-Controlled Chart Output Paths
low_findings.description: The skill includes examples that write chart files and create output directories from configurable output values. This is expected for a chart generator, but agents should avoid using untrusted absolute paths or parent-directory traversal values.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file-writing behavior is explicit and semantically relevant to chart export. It appears legitimate, but path values can come from caller-provided chart configuration.

low_findings.title: Command Execution Findings Are Markdown Fence False Positives
low_findings.description: The reported Ruby backtick execution locations are Markdown code block delimiters or starts and ends of documentation examples. No shell command execution or Ruby source code was found at these locations.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Every cited location is a Markdown fence or adjacent documentation boundary. The surrounding context contains chart examples, not executable shell or Ruby code.

low_findings.title: Environment Access Findings Are Dictionary Lookup False Positives
low_findings.description: The reported environment-access locations are calls to config.get in chart configuration dictionaries. No process.env, os.environ, dotenv loading, or secret-reading behavior was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code uses a local config dictionary for chart options. Searches found no environment-variable access primitives in the skill file.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography and Reconnaissance Findings Are Textual False Positives
low_findings.description: The reported blocker locations contain chart descriptions, responsive design guidance, best-practice text, or a grid comment. No cryptographic algorithm, hashing operation, host inspection, or system reconnaissance behavior was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines contain ordinary chart documentation. Additional keyword searches found no cryptography or reconnaissance APIs in SKILL.md.

dangerous_patterns.title: Caller-Controlled File Output
dangerous_patterns.description: Chart examples save files and create directories using caller-provided output names. This pattern is useful but should be constrained to the intended workspace.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The code directly shows output path construction and file saving. The pattern is legitimate for chart export, so severity remains low.

content.value_statement: Teams often need clear charts without choosing every visualization detail manually. This skill helps Claude, Codex, and Claude Code turn structured data into chart plans, code examples, exports, and dashboard layouts.
actual_capabilities: Recommends chart types for comparisons, trends, distributions, relationships, and composition.
Provides Python chart examples using Matplotlib, Seaborn, and Plotly.
Provides JavaScript chart examples using Chart.js rendering patterns.
Shows how to export charts as PNG, SVG, PDF, JPG, and interactive HTML.
Includes chart styling guidance for labels, legends, themes, annotations, and accessibility.
Outlines a pipeline for generating multiple charts from chart configuration data.
limitations: The skill is documentation and examples, not a packaged chart application.
Several snippets are illustrative and may need cleanup before direct execution.
It does not validate source data quality or statistical correctness automatically.
It does not provide built-in access control for generated output paths.

use_cases.title: Prepare Business Review Charts
use_cases.description: Turn sales, finance, or operations tables into clear comparison and trend charts for review meetings.

use_cases.title: Prototype Interactive Dashboards
use_cases.description: Create Plotly or Chart.js dashboard drafts before building a production reporting workflow.

use_cases.title: Improve Report Visuals
use_cases.description: Select readable chart types, labels, colors, and export formats for reports and publications.

prompt_templates.title: Choose a Chart Type
prompt_templates.prompt: Use chart-generator to review this dataset summary and recommend the best chart type. Explain the choice and list the required columns.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Static Chart
prompt_templates.prompt: Use chart-generator to create a Matplotlib chart for this CSV. Include a title, axis labels, readable colors, and a PNG export.

prompt_templates.title: Build an Interactive View
prompt_templates.prompt: Use chart-generator to design an interactive Plotly chart for these metrics. Include hover details, filters, and an HTML export plan.

prompt_templates.title: Generate a Chart Pipeline
prompt_templates.prompt: Use chart-generator to design a reusable pipeline that creates bar, line, scatter, and heatmap charts from this configuration table.

output_examples.input: A table with monthly revenue by product category.
output_examples.output: A grouped bar chart plan for category comparison, plus a line chart plan for monthly revenue trends.

output_examples.input: A dataset with customer age, income, region, and purchase value.
output_examples.output: A scatter plot recommendation using income and purchase value, with region as color and age as an optional size field.

output_examples.input: A correlation matrix for product usage metrics.
output_examples.output: A heatmap design with labeled axes, a diverging color scale, and a high-resolution export recommendation.

best_practices: Validate column names, units, and missing values before generating charts.
Use chart types that match the question, not only the available data.
Keep output files inside the project workspace and use clear file names.
anti_patterns: Do not use pie charts for many categories or precise comparisons.
Do not accept untrusted output paths without review.
Do not rely on default colors when accessibility or brand consistency matters.

faq.question: Can this skill create interactive charts?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes Plotly and Chart.js patterns for interactive HTML charts and dashboard-style views.

faq.question: Can it export charts for documents?
faq.answer: Yes. The examples cover PNG, SVG, PDF, and JPG exports for reports and presentations.

faq.question: Does it choose the best chart automatically?
faq.answer: It provides guidance based on data structure and visualization goals. A user should still confirm the business question.

faq.question: Does it clean or validate data?
faq.answer: No. It advises validation, but data cleaning must be handled before chart generation.

faq.question: Which libraries does it mention?
faq.answer: It covers Matplotlib, Seaborn, Plotly, and Chart.js examples.

faq.question: Is this safe to publish?
faq.answer: Yes. Review found low risk only, mainly because chart export examples write files to caller-specified paths.
